topic: Alignment - align_of and align_of_val at Compile and Runtime

turns.content: I'm reading through the std::mem documentation and I keep getting confused about when to use align_of versus align_of_val. My code is trying to access a dynamically sized array but I think I might be missing something about how alignment works for those types.

It's easy to get tripped up there because align_of::<T>() only works on concrete, fixed-size types at compile time, whereas align_of_val is needed when you have a value with an unknown size like [u8; 0] or &[T]. You mentioned dynamically sized arrays which means you need the runtime version. The key distinction is that size_of gives you the total byte width of the type while alignment tells you the strict boundary requirements for safe memory access. When dealing with atomics or SIMD instructions, this becomes critical because misalignment can cause undefined behavior even if the data fits in a cache line.
Oh I see now but wait, so if I have a Vec<u8> that's growing dynamically, does align_of::<Vec<u8>> actually tell me anything useful? Or is it just size_of that matters for memory layout here?

You've caught the exact misconception many students make - you cannot call align_of on Vec because it doesn't implement the trait for concrete types. For a growing vector, what really matters is how much padding exists between its data and nearby memory boundaries. In practice, when SIMD loads data from a Vec or uses atomics on dynamically sized buffers, the compiler often needs to know the alignment at runtime to avoid misaligned access errors. That's why align_of_val becomes essential for these cases instead of relying on compile-time constants like align_of does.

topic: Alignment - align_of and align_of_val at Compile and Runtime

turns.content:
I've read about std::mem::align_of::<T>() and align_of_val before, but I still don't fully grasp why we need a separate runtime function for dynamically-sized types when compile-time alignment seems to cover most cases.

The key distinction is that std::mem::align_of::<T>() only works because the type T has a fixed size known at compile time, whereas align_of_val requires knowing the runtime value of a pointer or slice. Think of it like this: if you are building a house with pre-fabricated blocks, you know exactly where each block fits before construction starts (compile-time), but if you are pouring concrete into forms that might change shape based on the load, you need to measure the actual volume at the moment of casting (runtime).
That analogy helped a bit. So when I have a slice or a pointer that might not be sized, how does align_of_val actually interact with the memory layout to ensure safety?

topic: Alignment - align_of and align_of_val at Compile and Runtime

turns.content: I'm reading through the section on memory layout in my book, specifically around std::mem::align_of::<T> and how it relates to size_of. I thought alignment was just a property of the data type itself like i32 or f64, so if I have an array of those types, shouldn't every element be perfectly aligned? But then I tried aligning some dynamically sized values at runtime using align_of_val and got unexpected results where my pointer arithmetic failed. Why does size_of not automatically give me the alignment needed for safe access on all platforms?

You made a common assumption that alignment is static to the type, but it's actually tied to the platform's ABI and can vary between architectures like x86_64 versus ARM. The std::mem::align_of::<T>() function returns the compile-time alignment requirement for a fixed-size type T, which is why your i32 array works predictably everywhere. However, when you deal with dynamically sized values using align_of_val, you're dealing with runtime information because the size and layout can change depending on how the data was constructed or cast. This matters deeply for atomics and SIMD operations where misalignment causes hardware exceptions or performance penalties, so understanding the difference between compile-time constraints via std::mem::align_of::<T> and runtime values accessed through align_of_val is crucial for writing safe Rust code that crosses platform boundaries.
